title: -Revenue Grant: FEMA Seismic Hazard Mitigation Grant Program

sections.recommendation:
Adopt a Resolution authorizing the City Manager to submit a nonbinding letter of
commitment of matching funds for a seismic Hazard Mitigation Grant application for the
City of Berkeley Civic Center Project-Seismic Retrofit of MSB and VMB to the Federal
Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), and subject to its award, to accept the grant,
execute any resultant revenue agreements and amendments, accept any additional
funding allocations from the Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP), and designate the
City Manager as the applicant’s authorized agent. If awarded, the grant will underwrite the
seismic retrofit of two historic and critical buildings in Berkeley’s Civic Center, the
Maudelle Shirek Building (“MSB” or “Old City Hall”) and the Veterans Memorial Building
(“VMB”).
sections.fiscal_impacts:
The City of Berkeley has been deemed eligible to apply for a grant of up to $16.5 million
as part of FEMA’s Hazard Mitigation Grant Program to seismically retrofit two historic
Civic Center buildings, the Maudelle Shirek Building (MSB) and the Veterans Memorial
Building (VMB), to address critical structural deficiencies and reduce collapse risk
during major earthquakes.
FEMA requires applicants to provide a letter committing to expend matching funds
equating to 25% of the award amount over the four-year (48 month) grant duration, for a
total project amount of $21.9 million. The City match portion of $5.4 million includes in-
kind expenditures for project management. The grant application includes a request for
grant management expenses, which do not require a local match. The projected grant
administrative costs of ($136,080.00) are well below the allowable 5%.
If awarded, the HMGP grant would provide nearly $16.5 million to the City of Berkeley to
seismically retrofit two historic Civic Center buildings, the Maudelle Shirek Building (MSB)
and the Veterans Memorial Building (VMB). These retrofits will transform both seismically
deficient structures into resilient community assets capable of withstanding design-level

earthquakes with only limited, repairable damage, preserving their historic fabric and
ensuring continuity of essential civic and community functions in Berkeley’s Civic Center.
The building-specific seismic retrofits include the following elements:
 The MSB (2134 Martin Luther King Jr Way), constructed in 1909, will be upgraded
to a Damage Control Plus (DC+) performance level through a 2,760 sq. ft.
reinforced concrete rear addition with 12-inch core walls, an 18-inch m
sections.current_situation:
The Berkeley Civic Center Project will seismically retrofit two historic and critical
buildings: the Maudelle Shirek Building (MSB, Old City Hall) and the Veterans Memorial
Building (VMB) located at the heart of Berkeley’s Civic Center. MSB, built in 1909,
predates seismic codes and last underwent major renovation in the 1970s, which
excluded seismic upgrades. VMB, built in 1928, similarly lacks seismic design and last
received rehabilitation in 1993 without structural strengthening. Both buildings lie less
than two miles from the Hayward Fault, and sustained impacts from the 1989 Loma
Prieta (M6.9) and 2014 South Napa (M6.0) earthquakes. Both buildings are seismically
deficient pre-code structures and do not meet the American Society of Civil Engineers
(ASCE) 41-17 Basic Performance Objectives. They pose life safety hazards and risk
partial or total collapse in a major earthquake.
The Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP) funds plans and projects that reduce the
effects of future natural disasters. HMGP grants are funding provided by the Federal
Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) to states. In California, these funds are
administered by the Cal OES (Office of Emergency Services) HMGP Unit. The HMGP
grant requires a 25% local match of the total award, and if granted would be significant
($16.5M). Awarded projects must be cost-effective, demonstrated by a benefit-cost
ratio (BCR) of 1.0 or greater. The City of Berkeley Civic Center Project – Seismic
Retrofit of MSB and VMB grant, will be submitted with a combined BCR over 2.0.
Benefit Cost Ratio – Standard (MSB): 1.55
Benefit Cost Ratio – Standard (VMB): 4.17
Benefit Cost Ratio – Standard (Total): 2.43

sections.background:
Berkeley's Civic Center is a National Register-listed historic district in Downtown
Berkeley, centering on Martin Luther King Jr. Civic Center Park and featuring significant
historic buildings like the Maudelle Shirek Building (formerly City Hall, or often referred
to as “Old City Hall”) and the Veterans Memorial Building. The area serves as a vital
public space for community events, and government functions, encompassing civic
buildings, green spaces, and educational facilities. Planning for the Civic Center is
guided by two documents both shaped by extensive stakeholder engagement; the Civic
1 City of Berkeley, Local Hazard Mitigation Plan or LHMP, page 49, B-41, adopted March 2025.
Page | 3
Page 4 of 11
Revenue Grant: FEMA Hazard Mitigation Grant Program CONSENT CALENDAR
October 14, 2025
Center Vision and Implementation Plan (adopted September 2020) and the Civic Center
Design Concept (adopted July 2023).2
After the adoption of the Civic Center Design Concept in 2023, the project moved to a
third phase, one that focuses on the rehabilitation of the buildings and the attainment of
funding for advancing the Vision Plan and Design Concept. The two structures,
Veterans Memorial Building and the Maudelle Shirek Building (VMB and MSB) in the
Civic Center were built in the first quarter of the 1900s and have not been updated to
current seismic standards. The HMGP grant would provide the City with necessary
financial support to complete the proposed seismic retrofit projects to enhance the
structural integrity of both the MSB and VMB to be able to withstand a seismic event
and mitigate against the hazard posed by an earthquake.
ENVIRONMENTAL SUSTAINABILITY AND CLIMATE IMPACTS
Both buildings will be less likely to suffer damage in the event of an earthquake, and as
a result will be less likely than non-retrofitted structures to create additional
environmental impacts from collapse. If the mitigation projects prevent the buildings
from collapse, that would reduce injuries
sections.rationale:
If the City receives funding under FEMA’s Seismic Hazard Mitigation Grant Program,
two remarkable and vulnerable structures in Berkeley’s Civic Center will be retrofitted,
preserving their historic fabric and ensuring continuity of essential civic and community
functions in the event of a major seismic event.
2 Civic Center Vision Statement: Civic Center will be the heart of Berkeley’s community. Civic Center will
be the prime space for civic life, culture, and the arts. It will reflect the city’s diverse identities, celebrate its
history, and contribute to shaping its future. A place of shared resources and a platform for free
expression accessible to all, Civic Center aims to manifest the city’s values, advance social justice, and
demonstrate the power of true public space. (Adopted by the Berkeley City Council, September 2020).

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###-N.S.
AUTHORIZING THE CITY MANAGER TO COMMIT MATCHING FUNDS FOR A
HAZARD MITIGATION GRANT APPLICATION FOR THE CIVIC CENTER SEISMIC
RETROFIT FOR THE MAUDELLE SHIREK AND VETERANS MEMORIAL BUILDINGS
TO THE FEDERAL EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT AGENCY (FEMA) AND SUBJECT
TO ITS AWARD, TO ACCEPT THE GRANT, EXECUTE ANY RESULTANT REVENUE
AGREEMENTS AND AMENDMENTS, ACCEPT ANY ADDITIONAL FUNDING
ALLOCATIONS FROM THE HAZARD MITIGATION GRANT PROGRAM AND
DESIGNATE THE APPLICANT’S DESIGNATED AGENT
WHEREAS, as a result of the declaration of a major federal disaster, the State of
California is eligible for Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP) funding aimed at
reducing or eliminating future damages; and
WHEREAS, federal funding is provided under the Robert T. Stafford Emergency
Assistance and Disaster Relief Act (Stafford Act) through FEMA and the California
Governor’s Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES), Cal OES is responsible for
identifying program priorities, reviewing applications and forwarding recommendations for
funding to FEMA, and FEMA has final approval for activity eligibility and funding; and
WHEREAS, the federal regulations governing the HMGP are found in Title 44 of the Code
of Federal Regulations (44 CFR) Part 206, and the Uniform Administrative Requirements
for Federal Awards are found in 2 CFR Part 200; and
WHEREAS, a Notice of Interest was approved for City of Berkeley Civic Center Project-
Seismic Retrofit of Maudelle Shirek Building and Veterans Memorial Building on May 30,
2025, and the City of Berkeley will submit a full application for HMGP funding on or before
September 15, 2025; and
WHEREAS, a letter committing at least 25% matching funds is required as part of the
grant application, with matching funds from a non-federal source; and
WHEREAS, the match will consist of $5.4 million over the four-year grant period, which
will come
